intro:
The Negev (Hebrew negev, “the dry land,” which became the ordinary word for “south”) is the arid southern region of Canaan — a wedge of steppe and desert running south from the hill country of Judah toward the wilderness of Paran and the gulf at Ezion-geber. A land of scarce water, its life gathered around wells and seasonal streams, and its chief town was Beersheba, the proverbial southern limit of the land in the phrase “from Dan to Beersheba” (Judges 20:1).

The Negev is above all the land of the patriarchs’ sojourning. Abram “journeyed on toward the Negev” (Genesis 12:9), dwelt at Gerar and Beersheba, and there dug a well and swore a covenant with Abimelech (Genesis 21:31). Hagar, fleeing, met the angel of the LORD at Beer-lahai-roi and called him “the God who sees” (Genesis 16:13–14), and Isaac later settled by that same well (Genesis 24:62). Isaac too dug wells and contended for water in the valleys of Gerar (Genesis 26:18–22), and at Beersheba the LORD appeared to him and renewed the covenant (Genesis 26:23–24); there too God spoke to Jacob as he went down to Egypt (Genesis 46:1–4).

At the threshold of the conquest the spies went up through the Negev to view the land (Numbers 13:22); when Israel presumed to go up without the LORD they were beaten back to Hormah (Numbers 14:45), though later they devoted its Canaanite cities to destruction (Numbers 21:1–3). In David’s outlaw years he raided from Ziklag, and after recovering the spoil from the Amalekites he sent gifts to the towns of the Negev (1 Samuel 30:26–27), whose forts long guarded Judah’s vulnerable south.

The Negev’s wells and covenants make it a fitting emblem of God’s faithfulness in a thirsty land — the place where the LORD met the castaway Hagar and named himself the God who sees. The prophets promised that its dry streambeds would run again (Psalms 126:4), a sign of the restoration the Messiah brings, who gives the thirsty the water of life without price and turns the desert into a garden (Isaiah 35:1).

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Beersheba
Well-town of the patriarchs’ covenants; the land’s southern marker (Gen 21:31)
Gerar
Town of Abimelech where Abraham and Isaac sojourned (Gen 26:1)
Beer-lahai-roi
‘Well of the Living One who sees me,’ where Hagar was met (Gen 16:14)
Arad
Negev city whose king attacked Israel near the conquest (Num 21:1)
Hormah
Where presuming Israel was routed, and later the ban fulfilled (Num 14:45)
Ziklag
David’s base, raided by the Amalekites and recovered (1 Sam 30:1)
Ezion-geber
Port at the Negev’s southern tip on the Gulf of Aqaba (1 Kgs 9:26)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places appear on the map above.

cells: Abraham
Sojourned through the Negev and dug the well at Beersheba (Gen 21:33)
Isaac
Dwelt by Beer-lahai-roi and dug the wells of Gerar (Gen 26:18)
Jacob
Met by God at Beersheba on his way down to Egypt (Gen 46:1)
Hagar
Met the angel at Beer-lahai-roi and was given a promise (Gen 16:13)
Ishmael
Grew up in the wilderness of the south, an archer (Gen 21:20–21)
David
Raided and ruled from Ziklag in the Negev (1 Sam 27:6)
